title: Nehemiah 4
subtitle: Opposition: From Ridicule to Armed Threat

heb.paragraph: Sanballat "ridiculed" (lāʿag) the Jews. Mockery is the first weapon. It costs nothing and demoralises without risk. Tobiah adds: "Even a fox would break down their wall." The insult targets the quality of the work — and by extension, the God behind it.

hist.historical: Sanballat mocks "in the presence of his associates and the army of Samaria" (v.2). This is a public political performance: the ridicule is designed to maintain his coalition’s confidence that the Jerusalem project will fail. The mockery is strategic, not spontaneous.
hist.context: Nehemiah’s response to ridicule is prayer, not retaliation: "Hear us, our God, for we are despised" (v.4). Then: "So we rebuilt the wall till all of it reached half its height, for the people worked with all their heart" (v.6). The best answer to mockery is progress.

refs.note: "We have endured no end of contempt and ridicule" — the psalm Nehemiah’s prayer echoes.
Rabshakeh’s mockery of Jerusalem — the Assyrian precedent for psychological warfare against the city.

notes.note: Sanballat’s five mocking questions: Can they rebuild? Will they sacrifice? Will they finish? Can they revive burned stones? What are they doing? Each question targets a different anxiety.
Tobiah: "Even a fox climbing on their wall would break it down." The insult is memorable because it’s clever. Tobiah has a gift for the cutting phrase.

Nehemiah’s prayer is imprecatory: "Turn their insults back on their own heads." He does not pray for their conversion but for their defeat. Imprecatory prayer expresses righteous anger before God rather than through retaliation.
The wall reaches half height "for the people worked with all their heart." Morale is the decisive factor. Mockery failed because the hearts were willing.
Calvin: "Tobiah mocked what God was building. Let the church remember: contempt from enemies is often the prelude to divine vindication."
Calvin on imprecatory prayer: "Nehemiah did not take revenge but committed his cause to God. This is the only lawful response to persecution: prayer, not retaliation."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "the army of Samaria" (ḥêl šōmərōn) indicates Sanballat commands military forces as governor — his mockery carries an implicit threat.
The NET observes the fox (šûʿāl) is the smallest and lightest animal that might climb ruins — Tobiah’s point is that the wall cannot bear even negligible weight.

williamson.source: H.G.M. Williamson, Ezra, Nehemiah, WBC (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Williamson reads Sanballat’s speech as political theatre performed before his military commanders. The mockery serves a dual purpose: demoralising Jerusalem and reassuring Samaria.
The imprecatory prayer is standard biblical lament form (cf. Ps 79:12; 109:6–15). Williamson notes Nehemiah channels anger through prayer rather than military action.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Ezra and Nehemiah, TOTC (1979) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "Tobiah’s fox is the perfect weapon of contempt — lightweight, ludicrous, unforgettable. But walls built in faith are not tested by foxes."
Kidner on "with all their heart": "The Hebrew idiom is lēḇ — heart, not hands. The wall rose because the will was strong, not because the technique was perfect."

sections.header: Verses 7—14 — Conspiracy to Attack; Nehemiah Arms the Builders

heb.paragraph: The opposition escalates from mockery to conspiracy to "fight against Jerusalem" (lāḥam). The shift from words to weapons marks a new phase. Nehemiah’s response also escalates: from prayer alone (v.4) to prayer plus military preparation (v.9).
"Remember the Lord, who is great and awesome, and fight" (v.14). The verb zāk̅ar (remember) is Nehemiah’s characteristic word — it appears throughout the book. Memory is the foundation of courage.

hist.historical: The coalition expands: Sanballat (north), Tobiah and Ammonites (east), Arabs (south), Ashdodites (west). Jerusalem is surrounded on all four sides. The conspiracy is coordinated — a multi-front military threat designed to make defence impossible.
hist.context: "We prayed to our God and posted a guard" (v.9). The sentence contains Nehemiah’s entire theology of action: pray AND act. Not prayer instead of guards. Not guards instead of prayer. Both. This is not a lack of faith but an expression of it — faith engages the means God provides.

refs.note: Jehoshaphat: "We do not know what to do, but our eyes are on you" — the prayer-then-fight pattern.
The armour of God — the NT theological framework for Nehemiah’s practical principle.

notes.note: Four-directional coalition: north, east, south, west. The geography is total encirclement. Nehemiah is surrounded but not contained.
"We prayed to our God and posted a guard." Eleven words that contain an entire theology of practical faith. Prayer without action is presumption; action without prayer is pride.
Nehemiah stations armed families "by clans, with their swords, spears and bows." The unit is the family. They fight for what they love — their homes, their children, their section of wall.
"Remember the Lord, who is great and awesome, and fight for your families." Theology and tactics in one sentence. Remember God; fight for your people. Both commands are necessary.
Calvin: "He prayed and set a watch. These two are not contradictory but complementary. God works through means, and faith uses them without relying on them."
Calvin: "He bade them remember the greatness of God in order to drive out fear. The more we know God, the less we fear men."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the double response (prayer + guard) is a practical outworking of the "trust and verify" principle: divine dependence expressed through human diligence.
The NET observes the deployment "by clans" (ləmišpəḥōtēhem) mirrors Israel’s military organisation in Numbers. Nehemiah uses Torah’s tribal structure for defence.

williamson.source: H.G.M. Williamson, Ezra, Nehemiah, WBC (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Williamson maps the four-directional threat: Sanballat from Samaria (north), Tobiah from Ammon (east), Arabs from the Negev (south), Ashdodites from the coastal plain (west). A coordinated stranglehold.
Williamson: "The combination of prayer and practical measures is characteristic of Nehemiah’s spirituality. He trusts God without testing God."

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Ezra and Nehemiah, TOTC (1979) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "Prayed and posted a guard. Six words, and not one too many. The order matters: prayer first, then the practical step. But both are present."
Kidner on "Remember the Lord and fight": "Nehemiah did not say ‘Remember the Lord and he will fight for you.’ He said ‘Remember the Lord AND fight.’ Faith arms the hand; it does not tie it."

heb.paragraph: "Half did the work while the other half held spears, shields, bows and armour" (v.16). The image of armed builders is Nehemiah’s signature: construction as combat, building as battle. The ḥănît (spear) in one hand and the trowel in the other.

refs.note: "Be on your guard; stand firm in the faith; be courageous; be strong" — the NT echo of Nehemiah’s armed builders.

notes.note: "When our enemies heard that we were aware of their plot, God had frustrated it." The conspiracy fails not through battle but through disclosure. Sometimes defence is simply alertness.
The division of labour: half build, half guard. Those who carry loads do so one-handed, weapon in the other. The builders wear swords at their sides. Every worker is also a soldier.

The trumpet system: because the builders are spread along a long wall, they need communication. Nehemiah establishes a rally point: wherever the trumpet sounds, gather there. Military communication protocol for a construction project.

"We worked from dawn till the stars came out." Extended hours under threat. "Neither I nor my brothers nor my men nor the guards with me took off our clothes." They slept in their work clothes, ready for action at any moment.
Calvin: "To build with one hand and hold a weapon with the other is the calling of every believer in this world. We are always building and always defending. Peace and war are simultaneous."
Calvin: "They did not undress. This total dedication teaches us that the work of God demands not convenience but commitment."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "with one hand" is literally "one of his hands was doing the work while the other held a weapon." The image is literary — some tasks require both hands — but the principle is clear.
The NET observes "each had his weapon, even when he went for water" suggests the threat was continuous, not intermittent. No moment was safe.

williamson.source: H.G.M. Williamson, Ezra, Nehemiah, WBC (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Williamson analyses the military arrangements as a combination of reserve force (half guarding) and individual readiness (each builder armed). The dual system is professional, not improvised.
The trumpet (shōpār) as rally signal follows the military pattern from Numbers 10:9. Nehemiah applies Torah’s warfare protocols to his construction project.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Ezra and Nehemiah, TOTC (1979) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "Trowel in one hand, sword in the other. This is the image the church has returned to in every age of opposition: build and defend simultaneously."
Kidner on sleeping in clothes: "They lived in a state of permanent readiness. Comfort was sacrificed for vigilance. This is what serious work looks like."

hist.context: The armed builder is the chapter’s defining image and one of the most iconic in the OT. "Those who carried materials did their work with one hand and held a weapon in the other, and each of the builders wore his sword at his side" (vv.17–18). The wall is built under threat. This is what faith looks like when the environment is hostile: you don’t stop building, but you don’t pretend there’s no danger.

ppl.name: Sanballat
ppl.role: Governor of Samaria; leads the mockery and conspiracy
ppl.text: His tactics escalate across the chapter: ridicule (vv.1–3), conspiracy (vv.7–8), then... the threat collapses (v.15). Each escalation is met by a combination of prayer and practical action.

ppl.name: Tobiah
ppl.role: Ammonite official; master of the cutting remark
ppl.text: "Even a fox would break down their wall" — the most quoted insult in the book. His wit makes him the more memorable opponent, though Sanballat holds the military power.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBut when Sanballat, Tobiah, the Arabs, the Ammonites and the people of Ashdod heard that the repairs to Jerusalem’s walls had gone ahead and that the gaps were being closed, they were very angry.ESVBut when Sanballat and Tobiah and the Arabs and the Ammonites and the Ashdodites heard that the repairing of the walls of Jerusalem was going forward and that the breaches were beginning to be closed, they were very angry.NIVDo not cover up their guilt or blot out their sins from your sight, for they have thrown insults in the face of the builders.ESVDo not cover their guilt, and let not their sin be blotted out from your sight, for they have provoked you to anger in the presence of the builders.

rec.text: "Sanballat’s five mocking questions: Can they rebuild? Will they sacrifice? Will they finish? Can they revive burned stones? What are they doing? Each question targets a different anxiety."

rows.label: Mockery — 4:1–6
rows.text: Words as weapons. Ridicule targets morale, not material. Prayer is the answer; progress is the proof.

rows.label: Armed Builders — 4:15–23
rows.text: Trowel and sword. Every worker also a soldier. Dawn to stars, sleeping in armour.

hebtext.note: Sanballat "ridiculed" (lāʿag) the Jews. Mockery is the first weapon. It costs nothing and demoralises without risk. Tobiah adds: "Even a fox would break down their wall." The insult targets the quality of the work — and by extension, the God behind it.

The opposition escalates from mockery to conspiracy to "fight against Jerusalem" (lāḥam). The shift from words to weapons marks a new phase. Nehemiah’s response also escalates: from prayer alone (v.4) to prayer plus military preparation (v.9).
"Remember the Lord, who is great and awesome, and fight" (v.14). The verb zāk̅ar (remember) is Nehemiah’s characteristic word — it appears throughout the book. Memory is the foundation of courage.

"Half did the work while the other half held spears, shields, bows and armour" (v.16). The image of armed builders is Nehemiah’s signature: construction as combat, building as battle. The ḥănît (spear) in one hand and the trowel in the other.

thread.text: "We have endured no end of contempt and ridicule" — the psalm Nehemiah’s prayer echoes.
Rabshakeh’s mockery of Jerusalem — the Assyrian precedent for psychological warfare against the city.
Jehoshaphat: "We do not know what to do, but our eyes are on you" — the prayer-then-fight pattern.
The armour of God — the NT theological framework for Nehemiah’s practical principle.
"Be on your guard; stand firm in the faith; be courageous; be strong" — the NT echo of Nehemiah’s armed builders.

themes.note: Faith peaks: "prayed and posted a guard" is the chapter’s thesis. Mission is high — the work continues under threat. The armed builder is the book’s iconic image.
